one_line_takeaway: CBV shows that diffusion-guided, GradCAM-masked, clean-label image poisoning can implant VLM backdoors with ASR (80-88%) rivaling dirty-label attacks while preserving natural-looking images and clean-task performance, evading label-mismatch-based detection and defeating tested defenses (Fine-pruning, TIJO).
key_finding: On image captioning (Table 2), CBV achieves ASR of 85.77% (LLaVA-v1.5-7B), 83.83% (MiniGPT-v2), 84.12% (Qwen-2.5-VL-7B), and 87.71% (InstructBLIP-7B), versus prior clean-label baselines that top out around 14.58% ASR (e.g., CBAVR 9.28/11.74/7.36/14.58% across the four models; Narcissus, HTBA, SAA, IP all in the 6-13% range). CBV's ASR is comparable to dirty-label attacks (e.g., TrojVLM 94.60/89.11/93.79/88.45%) while clean-task quality (BLEU@4, CIDEr) stays close to the no-attack baseline (e.g., LLaVA CIDEr 121.08 for CBV vs 131.20 clean vs 121.28 for TrojVLM). On VQA (Table 3), CBV reaches 84.89/85.87/83.58/85.55% ASR across the four models with VQA Accuracy staying within a few points of clean (e.g., 74.86% vs 78.21% clean for LLaVA), again far above clean-label baselines (5.40-13.54% ASR) and comparable to dirty-label methods. Ablations: multimodal text guidance raises ASR from 74.47% to 85.77% (LLaVA) and 72.09% to 83.83% (MiniGPT-v2) on VQA (Table 4). The learned UAP trigger outperforms patch and noise triggers (84.27% vs 78.40% vs 74.16% ASR on MSCOCO; Table 5). GradCAM masking (GM) improves stealth metrics (LPIPS 23.78 vs 26.52, SSIM 0.7139 vs 0.5511 without GM; Table 6). CBV's generation is far cheaper computationally than other clean-label/optimization-based methods: 28.7s ± 1.9 average per sample vs 185.4-218.3s for HTBA, SAA, Shadowcast, Narcissus, and IP (Table 7). Poisoning-rate sweep shows ASR ~60% at 2% poisoning rate and ~80% at 4%, with clean performance degrading as the rate increases further, motivating the 5% default. Against defenses: Fine-pruning up to 10% pruning ratio does not reduce ASR (while starting to hurt clean accuracy); TIJO detects only ~30% (AUC) of CBV's backdoors, far below full detection.
core_contribution: The paper proposes CBV (Clean-label Backdoor attack on VLMs via diffusion models), a data-poisoning attack that produces clean-label poisoned image-text training samples for Vision-Language Models. Unlike prior VLM backdoor attacks that modify text labels (making poisoned samples easy to detect via image-text mismatch), CBV keeps labels identical to clean samples and only perturbs the image. The pipeline has three stages: (1) generate a reusable Universal Adversarial Perturbation (UAP) as the trigger by projected gradient ascent that maximizes a CLIP image-text misalignment loss over benign images; (2) compute a GradCAM-guided Mask (GM) from a surrogate image classifier to localize the most semantically salient region of the target image, restricting subsequent edits to that region; (3) run a diffusion-model (Stable Diffusion v1.4) reverse denoising process on the masked region, where the score function at each step is modified with multimodal guidance terms -- gradients of CLIP image-image and image-text similarity between the current denoised estimate and the triggered image / its caption -- to steer generation toward embedding the trigger's semantic features while keeping the sample visually natural. A theoretical analysis (Bayes' rule + score-matching/denoising equivalence) is given to justify why this guided score embeds triggered-image semantics into the poisoned sample.
threat_model: Black-box data-poisoning threat model, explicitly stated in Section 3: the attacker publishes a poisoned dataset on a public repository; victims download it and train/fine-tune a VLM on it, unknowingly implanting a backdoor. The attacker has no knowledge of and no access to the victim model's architecture, parameters, or training procedure (cannot influence training), and only controls the released dataset. Attack goal has three objectives: (1) effectiveness -- backdoored model outputs an attacker-chosen semantic substitution (paper uses Human-to-Dog substitution) when a triggered image is presented; (2) functionality-preserving -- clean-input performance comparable to a normally trained model; (3) naturalness -- poisoned samples must look natural to evade human/manual inspection, distinguishing this from prior clean-label methods that only bound Lp pixel distance (which the paper shows still leaves visible noise/artifacts).
limitations: The paper's own scope limits: (1) evaluated only on a single attack semantic (Human-to-Dog substitution), with other targets deferred to an appendix sweep rather than the main results; (2) relies on CLIP as a surrogate for both UAP generation and diffusion guidance, and on a separate surrogate classifier for GradCAM masks -- the paper argues this shows transferability since no surrogate VLM is used, but all reported victim models are open-source 7B-scale VLMs, not proprietary/closed models; (3) STRIP and Neural Cleanse defense results are deferred to the appendix and not shown in the excerpted main text; (4) the method requires diffusion-based image generation per poisoned sample (though the paper reports this is cheaper than prior clean-label optimization baselines, it still adds a generation pipeline dependency, e.g. Stable Diffusion v1.4); (5) as with other data-poisoning backdoor work, the threat model assumes the victim trains/fine-tunes directly on the distributed dataset without additional data-quality filtering beyond what the tested defenses (Fine-pruning, TIJO) provide.

quantitative_results: Best prior clean-label baseline ASR on captioning tops out at 14.58% (CBAVR, InstructBLIP-7B); others (Narcissus, HTBA, SAA, IP) in 6-13% range across models/tasks
Clean (no-attack) CIDEr baselines: 131.20 (LLaVA), 119.61 (MiniGPT-v2), 140.33 (Qwen-2.5-VL-7B), 132.43 (InstructBLIP-7B); CBV CIDEr stays close: 121.08/112.16/136.67/131.31 respectively
Text/multimodal guidance ablation (VQA): ASR rises from 74.47% to 85.77% (LLaVA-v1.5-7B) and 72.09% to 83.83% (MiniGPT-v2) when adding text guidance
Trigger-type ablation on MSCOCO: UAP trigger ASR 84.27% vs Patch 78.40% vs Noise 74.16%
GradCAM-guided Mask (GM) ablation (MSCOCO): LPIPS 23.78 with GM vs 26.52 without; SSIM 0.7139 with GM vs 0.5511 without
Average poisoned-sample generation time: CBV 28.7s (±1.9) vs HTBA 185.4s, SAA 198.7s, Shadowcast 205.2s, Narcissus 212.6s, IP 218.3s
Poisoning-rate sweep: ~60% ASR at 2% poisoning rate, ~80% ASR at 4% (default used: 5%)
Fine-pruning defense: CBV attack remains unaffected up to 10% pruning ratio while clean accuracy begins to degrade
TIJO defense: detects only ~30% AUC of CBV's backdoors (reported as 21.24% on MSCOCO and 28.3% on VQA v2 per Fig. 8b)
baselines_compared: Dirty-label VLM backdoor attacks: TrojVLM, BadToken, MABA, VL-Trojan, Shadowcast
Clean-label backdoor attacks (originally for image/video classification, adapted here): CBAVR, Narcissus, HTBA, SAA (Sleeper Agent), IP (Invisible Poison)
